questions.question: Match each proposal section to what it must convince the reader of.
categories: You've done your homework
This is correct and replicable
We need this study
questions.explanation: Motivation convinces the reader 'we need this study', background shows 'you've done your homework' via the state of the art and credible sources, and methods convince that the design is correct and replicable.

questions.question: Match each of the 5 C's to its definition.
categories: What sources agree on
What sources disagree on or do differently
Which arguments were more convincing and what is lacking
How it applies to your own research

correct_mapping.Contrast: What sources disagree on or do differently
correct_mapping.Critique: Which arguments were more convincing and what is lacking
correct_mapping.Connect: How it applies to your own research

questions.explanation: Compare is agreement, Contrast is disagreement, Critique judges the arguments and gaps, and Connect links the literature to your own research. The fifth C, Cite, pairs an in-text citation with a matching reference entry.

questions.question: Match each AI use to the reason it is responsible or forbidden under the course policy.
items: Help with coding you already know
categories: Forbidden — AI invents facts
Forbidden — AI hallucinates the source
Responsible — you can verify it
Responsible — supports your process
questions.explanation: Brainstorming supports your process and code help is fine when you can verify it, so both are responsible. Generating content to submit is forbidden because AI invents facts, and finding sources is forbidden because AI hallucinates the source and its contents.

questions.question: Match each source-misuse error to its description.
categories: Reader can't tell which claim maps to which source
Reader assumes it's your own idea, risking plagiarism
Repetitive, no narrative, no point of your own

correct_mapping.Lumping citations at paragraph end: Reader can't tell which claim maps to which source
correct_mapping.Claim with no citation: Reader assumes it's your own idea, risking plagiarism
correct_mapping.Listing instead of synthesising: Repetitive, no narrative, no point of your own

questions.explanation: Lumping citations hides which claim maps to which source; an uncited claim is read as your own idea and risks plagiarism; listing is repetitive with no narrative or point of your own. The last is the most common and hardest to fix.

questions.question: Match each synthesis-matrix observation to what it means.
categories: Drop the idea or find more sources
The idea is too big; split it
One source at a time, a summary
One idea across sources, a synthesis

correct_mapping.A mostly-empty row: Drop the idea or find more sources
correct_mapping.A very full row: The idea is too big; split it
correct_mapping.A vertical read: One source at a time, a summary
correct_mapping.A horizontal read: One idea across sources, a synthesis

questions.explanation: An empty row signals weak support (drop it or find more sources); a full row signals an idea that is too big to split. Reading vertically gives a per-source summary, while reading horizontally gives synthesis across sources.

questions.question: Match the document to its primary goal.
categories: Convince the reader to approve a future study
Convince the reader the conducted study was good and contributes
Declare any GenAI or tooling used

correct_mapping.Proposal: Convince the reader to approve a future study
correct_mapping.Paper: Convince the reader the conducted study was good and contributes
correct_mapping.Technology Statement: Declare any GenAI or tooling used

questions.explanation: A proposal aims to get a future study approved; a paper argues a finished study was good and contributes to science and the real world; the Technology Statement is the required declaration of GenAI or tooling used.
